title: Hedging
subtitle: Sound certain of the uncertain — the language of careful claims
intro: Hedging is the language of critical thinking. It lets you advance a claim while signalling the strength of the evidence behind it — 'This may indicate…' / 'It appears that…' / 'The findings tend to suggest…'. Band 6 writers over-generalise ('Everyone knows that…', 'It is obvious that…'); Band 8 writers calibrate. Hedging is one of the fastest ways to sound academically mature.

rules.heading: Modal hedges
rules.body: may, might, could, would, should. 'Early intervention may reduce long-term costs.' Weaker than 'will', stronger than 'possibly'.

rules.heading: Lexical verb hedges
rules.body: appear, seem, tend to, suggest, indicate, point to. 'The data appear to suggest a seasonal pattern.'

rules.heading: Adverbial hedges
rules.body: possibly, probably, arguably, apparently, largely, broadly, in many cases, to some extent, perhaps. 'The policy is arguably the most effective of its kind.'

rules.heading: Noun hedges and approximators
rules.body: likelihood, possibility, tendency, assumption, evidence. 'There is growing evidence that screen time impairs sleep.' Also 'a certain amount of', 'a tendency towards', 'something of a…'.

examples.sentence: Early exposure to foreign languages appears to correlate with stronger cognitive flexibility in later life.
examples.note: Verb hedge 'appears to' + noun hedge 'correlate with'.

examples.sentence: Although the evidence is largely anecdotal, there is a growing suspicion that social media platforms contribute to adolescent anxiety.
It would be premature to conclude that automation alone is responsible for rising inequality.
Such measures tend to succeed where public trust is already high.
One could argue that the policy has achieved its short-term goals, though its long-term impact remains unclear.
examples.note: 'One could argue… though…' — classic academic balance.

band8_marker: Band 8 writers never write 'Everyone agrees…' or 'It is a well-known fact that…'. They replace these with calibrated claims: 'There is broad consensus that…', 'The prevailing view holds that…', 'While the evidence is not conclusive, most analysts accept that…'. This vocabulary of careful attribution — not mere politeness — is the voice examiners associate with C1/C2 proficiency.

common_errors.error: Over-confident claims: 'It is obvious that pollution causes cancer.'
common_errors.fix: Hedge: 'A growing body of evidence suggests a link between pollution and cancer.'

common_errors.error: Hedging and then contradicting: 'It is certainly possible that…'
common_errors.fix: Pick a stance: 'It is certain that…' (strong) OR 'It is possible that…' (hedged).

common_errors.error: Over-hedging to the point of evasion: 'It might perhaps possibly be somewhat true that…'
common_errors.fix: One hedge per claim is enough.

practice.title: Hedge the claim
practice.instruction: Rewrite each over-confident claim with appropriate hedging.

items.prompt: Everyone knows that technology makes children lazy.
items.answer: It has been argued that technology may reduce children’s physical activity, although the evidence is not conclusive.
items.explanation: Three hedges replace a sweeping claim.

items.prompt: This proves that the policy failed.
items.answer: This suggests that the policy may not have achieved its intended goals.

items.prompt: All young people today are addicted to their phones.
items.answer: A significant proportion of young people appear to exhibit signs of phone dependence.

items.prompt: The tax definitely helped the economy.
items.answer: The tax appears to have contributed to economic improvement, though other factors may also be relevant.

items.prompt: It is a fact that air pollution kills people.
items.answer: A considerable body of evidence indicates that air pollution is associated with premature mortality.

items.prompt: Obviously, remote work reduces productivity.
items.answer: Some studies suggest that remote work may, in certain contexts, reduce productivity.

items.prompt: Parents always want the best for their children.
items.answer: Most parents tend to prioritise their children’s welfare.

items.prompt: Climate change is 100% caused by humans.
items.answer: The overwhelming scientific consensus attributes recent climate change largely to human activity.

items.prompt: A) 'Maybe vaccines work.' B) 'Vaccination has been shown to reduce the incidence of infectious disease.'
A) 'Some people say that AI will take all jobs.' B) 'It has been speculated that AI could displace a substantial proportion of existing jobs.'
A) 'This is probably good.' B) 'This approach appears to offer several advantages.'
A) 'It is obvious that smoking is dangerous.' B) 'There is overwhelming evidence that smoking is harmful to health.'
A) 'We think this is a better solution.' B) 'This approach may well prove more effective.'
items.explanation: 'May well' is a finely tuned probability hedge.

items.prompt: A) 'No one knows what will happen next.' B) 'The long-term consequences remain uncertain.'
A) 'Definitely, social media causes anxiety.' B) 'A growing body of research links heavy social-media use to elevated anxiety.'
A) 'It’s true that cities are overcrowded.' B) 'Many major cities exhibit symptoms of overcrowding.'

practice.title: Supply a hedge
practice.instruction: Fill in with a hedge word or phrase.

items.prompt: The data ___ to suggest a correlation between diet and mental health.
It is ___ the case that urbanisation exacerbates inequality.
There is ___ evidence that commuting time affects life satisfaction.
Policy-makers ___ wish to consider the long-term implications before acting.
The effect of the reform ___ be limited in scope.
Such claims ___ be supported by the available evidence.
items.answer: cannot always / may not / do not necessarily

items.prompt: It would be ___ to conclude that the policy has failed entirely.
In ___ cases, early intervention proves decisive.
items.answer: many / most / certain / a number of
